title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating People
meta_description: Discover how to trust your gut feelings when evaluating people. Learn to distinguish intuition from fear and make better decisions about who to trust.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating People
og_description: Discover how to trust your gut feelings when evaluating people. Learn to distinguish intuition from fear and make better decisions about who to trust.
body:
You've Been Trusting People Wrong Your Whole Life

Let me guess. You're the person who walks into a room and knows something's off before anyone says a word. You meet someone new, and your stomach clenches for no reason you can name. Or maybe you feel an instant warmth, a sense of safety, and later find out they're actually a solid person. But then you doubt yourself. You second-guess. You tell yourself you're being judgmental, paranoid, or just plain weird.

Nothing is wrong with you. You're not broken. You're just calibrated for a wider range of information than most people. Your nervous system picks up on things that don't have words yet. That's not a flaw - it's a survival trait that's been labeled a problem because most of the world operates on logic, facts, and observable data.

Here's the thing nobody tells you: your intuition about people is usually right. The problem is you don't know how to trust it or what to do with it. You've been taught to override your gut with your head, to give people the benefit of the doubt until they prove you wrong. But that approach leaves you exhausted, confused, and sometimes burned.

So let's talk about how to actually use your intuition when evaluating people - without the guilt, without the overthinking, and without feeling like you're being unfair.

What Intuition Really Is (And Isn't)

First, let's clear something up. Intuition isn't magic. It's not psychic prediction where you know someone's entire life story after shaking their hand. It's not a crystal ball that tells you if they're lying about their job or their marriage.

Intuition is pattern recognition happening below your conscious awareness. Your brain has been collecting data about people since the day you were born. Every face, every tone of voice, every micro-expression, every slight shift in body language - it all gets filed away. When you meet someone new, your brain compares them to every person you've ever known. It runs through thousands of data points in milliseconds and spits out a feeling. That feeling is your intuition.

Does that land? Good. Because once you understand that intuition is just your brain doing its job, you stop feeling like you're making things up. You start seeing it as valid information.

But here's where it gets tricky. Your intuition about people can get clouded by your own stuff. Your past trauma, your fears, your unhealed wounds - they can all hijack the signal. So the goal isn't to blindly follow every feeling. The goal is to learn to tell the difference between a clean intuitive hit and a fear-based reaction.

The Difference Between Intuition and Fear

This is where most people get stuck. You feel something about someone, but you can't tell if it's real or if you're just being scared. Here's how to tell the difference.

Intuition is calm and neutral. It doesn't come with a story. It's just a knowing. You might feel a quiet "no" in your chest when someone offers you a ride home. It's not dramatic. It's not panicked. It's just there, like a gentle hand on your shoulder.

Fear is loud and urgent. Fear comes with a narrative. It says things like "What if they're dangerous?" or "They seem nice but I bet they're fake." Fear spins stories. Intuition states facts.

Intuition feels like clarity. You don't have to convince yourself. You don't need evidence. You just know.

Fear feels like confusion. You go back and forth. You analyze. You try to figure out why you feel the way you do. Intuition doesn't need a reason.

So when you're trying to evaluate someone, start by noticing: Is this feeling calm and clear, or is it anxious and story-driven? If it's the second one, take a step back. Your fear might be talking, not your intuition.

"Your intuition about people is usually right. The problem is you've been trained to override it with logic. When you feel something, don't explain it away. Sit with it. It's telling you something your conscious mind hasn't caught up to yet."

The Body Check: Your First Line of Defense

Your body is smarter than your brain when it comes to people. Your brain can be fooled by words, charm, and good looks. Your body knows the truth. So when you meet someone new, do a quick body check. Here's what to look for.

Your chest: Does it feel open and relaxed, or tight and closed? An open chest usually means safety. A tight chest means something's off.

Your stomach: Do you feel a sense of ease, or is there a knot? Your gut is literally called your second brain for a reason. Trust it.

Your shoulders: Are they relaxed or hunched? Tension in your shoulders can mean you're bracing for something.

Your breathing: Is it deep and easy, or shallow and quick? Shallow breathing around someone is a red flag.

This takes practice. Most of us are so disconnected from our bodies that we don't notice these signals until they're screaming at us. Start small. When you're around someone you already trust, notice what your body feels like. Then when you're around someone who makes you uncomfortable, notice the difference. Your body will teach you more than any advice I can give.

Red Flags vs. Yellow Flags

Not every uncomfortable feeling is a reason to run. Sometimes you're just picking up on someone's nervousness, their personality style, or a cultural difference. That's where red flags and yellow flags come in.

Red flags are dealbreakers. They're the things you can't ignore. Someone who lies to your face. Someone who pushes your boundaries. Someone who makes you feel small. When you see a red flag, you don't need more data. You need to leave.

Yellow flags are caution signs. They're things that make you pause. Someone who's a little too eager. Someone who talks about themselves constantly. Someone who seems to have a lot of drama in their life. Yellow flags don't mean you should cut someone off. They mean you should slow down and gather more information before you trust them.

Here's the mistake most sensitive people make: they treat every yellow flag like a red flag and every red flag like a yellow flag. They either overreact or underreact. The key is learning to tell the difference.

When you feel a yellow flag, ask yourself: "What would I need to see or hear from this person to feel safe?" Then give them a chance to show you. But if they keep showing you the same yellow flags, eventually those become red flags. People who don't change don't deserve unlimited chances.

The One-Week Rule for New People

Here's a practical tool I use with every new person I meet. I call it the One-Week Rule. When you meet someone new, don't make any big decisions about them for at least a week. Don't decide they're your new best friend. Don't decide they're a liar. Just observe.

During that week, pay attention to three things:

  • Consistency: Do their words match their actions? Do they say one thing and do another?
  • Energy: How do you feel after you spend time with them? Energized or drained? Lighter or heavier?
  • Boundaries: Do they respect your no? Do they push when you pull back?

This rule saves you from two common mistakes. First, it stops you from rushing into trust with someone who hasn't earned it. Second, it stops you from writing someone off based on one bad first impression. Give yourself a week of neutral observation. Then let your intuition speak.

And here's the thing - your intuition will be clearer after a week because you've given it data to work with. You can't intuit something from nothing. You need to interact with someone, watch them in different situations, see how they treat other people. Your intuition needs fuel. The One-Week Rule gives it that fuel.

When Your Intuition Conflicts with What People Tell You

This is the hardest part. Someone tells you they're a good person. Everyone else loves them. They have a great resume, a charming smile, and all the right words. But your gut says no. Now what?

First, don't ignore your gut. Ever. I don't care how many people vouch for them. I don't care how much evidence you have that they're great. If your gut says no, there's a reason. You might not know the reason yet. You might never know. But your gut picked up on something your conscious mind missed.

Second, don't act on your gut immediately either. You don't have to accuse them of anything. You don't have to tell everyone you think they're shady. Just hold the feeling. Keep it in your pocket. Let it inform your decisions without controlling them.

Here's what that looks like in practice: You meet someone new. Your gut says no. So you don't give them your phone number. You don't invite them into your home. You don't share personal information. You keep things surface-level until you have more data. If your gut turns out to be wrong, you haven't lost anything. If your gut turns out to be right, you've protected yourself.

This is the balanced approach that most sensitive people never learn. You don't have to be paranoid. You don't have to be naive. You just have to listen to your gut and let it guide your boundaries.

How to Test Your Intuition About Someone

Intuition isn't infallible. It gets better with practice. Here are a few ways to test your intuitive hits and get more accurate over time.

Write it down. When you meet someone new, write down your first impression. Don't edit it. Just write what you feel. Then come back to it later and see if you were right. This builds trust in your own perception.

Ask your body. Before you make a decision about someone, close your eyes and ask your body a yes-or-no question. "Is this person safe?" "Can I trust them?" "Should I work with them?" Notice what your body does. A yes usually feels like expansion or lightness. A no feels like contraction or heaviness.

Check for patterns. Look back at your history. Who were you right about? Who were you wrong about? What did you miss? This isn't about beating yourself up. It's about calibrating your internal compass.

Use small tests. If you're unsure about someone, give them a small opportunity to show you who they are. Ask for a small favor and see if they follow through. Share a small piece of personal information and see if they respect it. People reveal themselves in small moments.

If you want to go deeper with this, there are some great resources out there. One book that really helped me understand the mechanics of intuition is The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker. It's not a fluffy spiritual book - it's a hard-hitting look at how your survival instincts work and why you should trust them. You can check it out on Amazon here (paid link). It's one of those books that will change how you see every person you meet.

When to Trust Your Intuition and When to Question It

Your intuition is most reliable when you're grounded. When you're well-rested, calm, and centered, your intuitive signals are clear. When you're stressed, tired, hungry, or triggered, your signals get scrambled. So before you trust a feeling about someone, check your own state first.

Your intuition is also more reliable with people you've known for a while. The longer you've known someone, the more data your brain has to work with. If you've known someone for years and suddenly get a bad feeling, pay attention. That's different from getting a bad feeling about a stranger in a coffee shop.

Your intuition is least reliable when you're projecting. If someone reminds you of an ex, a parent, or someone who hurt you, your intuition might be responding to the past, not the present. In those cases, slow down. Ask yourself: "Is this feeling about this person, or is it about someone they remind me of?"

This is where self-awareness matters. The more you heal your own wounds, the clearer your intuition becomes. If you haven't done the work to separate your past from your present, your intuition will always be mixed with your trauma. That's not a reason to ignore your feelings. It's a reason to keep doing your inner work.

What to Do When You Get a Strong "No" About Someone

Okay, so you've done the work. You've checked your body. You've ruled out fear. You've given it time. And you still get a strong "no" about someone. Now what?

First, honor it. Don't try to talk yourself out of it. Don't let anyone else talk you out of it. Your "no" is valid. You don't need a reason that sounds good to anyone else. "I don't feel right about it" is a complete sentence.

Second, adjust your boundaries accordingly. If you can, limit your contact with this person. If you can't avoid them entirely, keep your interactions short and surface-level. Don't share vulnerable information. Don't put yourself in situations where you're dependent on them.

Third, don't feel guilty. You're not being mean. You're not being judgmental. You're protecting your energy. That's your job. No one else is going to do it for you.

If you struggle with guilt around setting boundaries, I'd recommend Boundaries by Henry Cloud and John Townsend. It's a classic for a reason. You can find it on Amazon here (paid link). It will help you feel less guilty about protecting yourself.

How to Stay Open Without Being Naive

This is the balance every sensitive person has to learn. You don't want to close yourself off from everyone. You want to stay open to connection, love, and new relationships. But you also don't want to get burned.

The key is to stay open but not vulnerable. Open means you're willing to see who someone really is. Open means you give them a chance to show up. Open means you don't pre-judge everyone as dangerous. Vulnerable means you hand them your trust before they've earned it. Vulnerable means you ignore red flags because you want to see the best in people. Open means you see clearly. Vulnerable means you see through rose-colored glasses.

Practice staying open while keeping your boundaries strong. You can be friendly without being close. You can be kind without being available. You can be warm without being trusting. It's a skill, and it takes practice.

One book that really helped me with this balance is The Power of Now by Eckhart Tolle. It's not specifically about people, but it teaches you how to stay present in your body, which is exactly where your intuition lives. You can grab it on Amazon here (paid link). Being present is the foundation of clear intuition.

The Hard Truth About Intuition and People

Here's what no one tells you. Your intuition about people will sometimes be wrong. You'll misread someone. You'll trust someone you shouldn't have. You'll push away someone who was actually safe. That's part of being human. It doesn't mean your intuition is broken. It means you're learning.

The goal isn't to be right 100% of the time. The goal is to get better over time. To learn from your mistakes. To calibrate your internal compass so it gets more accurate the more you use it.

And here's the other hard truth: some people are just hard to read. Narcissists, sociopaths, and skilled manipulators are designed to fool you. They're good at what they do. If you get fooled by one of them, that's not a failure of your intuition. It's a testament to how good they are at hiding. Don't beat yourself up.

Instead, learn the lesson. What did you miss? What signals did you ignore? What did your body tell you that you talked yourself out of? Use that information to get sharper next time.

If you want to understand the darker side of human nature better, The Sociopath Next Door by Martha Stout is an eye-opening read. Check it out on Amazon here (paid link). It will help you recognize the patterns that your intuition is already picking up on.

Your Intuition Is a Muscle. Use It or Lose It.

Like any skill, intuition gets stronger with practice. The more you use it, the more you trust it, the more accurate it becomes. The more you ignore it, override it, or second-guess it, the weaker it gets.

So start today. The next time you meet someone, do a body check. Notice
